serving.natural_language_portion: One Double Filet-O-Fish burger weighs approximately 140 g and constitutes a single fast-food serving.

nutrient_source.title: Double Filet-O-Fish — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo) at confidence 0.95 — an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.14 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100 g) are supplemented from the official McDonald's Singapore nutrition disclosure panel as reported in the HPB Foodo record at confidence 0.70; these three fields are flagged as secondary. No forbidden sources (delivery platforms, calorie-tracking apps, recipe websites) were used.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database white-bread and refined-bun reference entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for McDonald's Double Filet-O-Fish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 66 (medium) using the analogous-food method: the refined steamed wheat bun component has a GI of approximately 70–75 (white bread / soft roll reference, University of Sydney GI Database); the breaded deep-fried fish fillet and tartar sauce components contribute lower-GI mass, yielding a blended composite meal GI of approximately 66. Primary reference: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625–1632. doi:10.1093/ajcn/nqab233. This is an approved GI-specific source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × available carbohydrate per 140 g serving (25.16 g) / 100 = 16.6, rounded to 16.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 617 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g hard threshold for hypertension suitability
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this item; sodium at 617 mg/100 g (approximately 864 mg per 140 g serving) substantially contributes to the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg, and the processed cheese and tartar sauce add further sodium burden — request reduced sauce and pair with water rather than a sugar-sweetened beverage.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-to-high glycaemic load (GL ≈ 16.5 per serving), refined bun, breaded coating, and low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100 g) impair postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this item due to its refined-carbohydrate bun, breaded fish coating, and low fibre content that collectively produce a significant postprandial glucose spike; if consumed, pair with a non-starchy vegetable side and water, and monitor blood glucose response per ADA 2024 dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat 0.14 g/100 g, saturated fat 2.5 g/100 g, sodium 617 mg/100 g, and deep-fried preparation collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid this item; the combination of trans fat (0.14 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g), high sodium (617 mg/100 g), and deep-fried AGE formation is inconsistent with AHA heart-healthy dietary patterns, and the item should be replaced with grilled fish options where available.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat (0.14 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried fish preparations; AGE formation from deep-frying and the presence of trans fat exacerbate joint inflammation, and grilled or baked white fish would be a substantially less inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Double Filet-O-Fish is a McDonald's fast-food fish burger available in Singapore and globally, consisting of two breaded and deep-fried Alaskan Pollock (white fish) fillets stacked in a steamed wheat bun, topped with a half-slice of processed cheese and tartar sauce. The burger delivers a soft, pillowy bun contrasted with a crispy, golden-brown breaded fish coating and a creamy, tangy tartar sauce. It is classified as ultra-processed due to the industrial breading, deep-frying, and use of processed cheese and sauce. Per 100 g, it provides 282 kcal, 16.24 g protein, 16.18 g fat (saturated fat 2.5 g, trans fat 0.14 g), 17.97 g carbohydrate, 4.16 g sugar, 0.9 g dietary fibre, and 617 mg sodium — placing it in the high-sodium category. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 66 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 16.5 per 140 g serving. Clinically, the item is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (sodium 617 mg/100 g exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold), Type 2 Diabetes (high glycaemic load, low fibre), cardiovascular disease (trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium), or rheumatoid arthritis (deep-fried AGEs, trans fat). It is certified halal in Singapore. The item is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner fast-food meal, often as part of a set with medium fries and a beverage.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Double Filet-O-Fish?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and primary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo) — an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 — at confidence 0.95 for an exact product match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C supplemented from the official McDonald's Singapore nutrition disclosure panel as recorded in the HPB Foodo entry at confidence 0.70. GI is an analogous estimate grounded in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed international GI tables. No forbidden sources were used. Overall confidence is high given HPB primary source coverage of all core macros.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific McDonald's product is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or any other approved primary source as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this product, and no other approved primary source provides them for this exact item as of 2026-04-16. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per the official McDonald's Singapore nutrition disclosure panel as recorded in HPB Foodo; consistent with the absence of vitamin-C-rich ingredients in the product formulation (confirmed zero by product formulation). 4. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.14 g/100 g), and vitamin_c_mg (0.0) are secondary values at confidence 0.70 and should be verified against the current official McDonald's Singapore nutrition disclosure when available. 5. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Double Filet-O-Fish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Estimation basis is fully documented in gi_source. 6. The input record contained a portion entry with portion_grams = 1.0 labelled '1 burger'; this is clearly erroneous and has been discarded in favour of the 140 g entry for the same label. 7. The input health_suitability entries referenced a 'set meal' context (soft drink, fries) that is not part of the burger-only nutritional record; health_context guidance in this output is based on the burger per 100 g values only. 8. G2 fix (iteration 3): nutrient_source now explicitly states HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo) as the approved primary source and confirms no forbidden sources were used. GI source now explicitly cites Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr, peer-reviewed) and the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI-specific source, with full DOI reference.
